event: Early hunter-gatherers occupy the rock shelter of El Abra near present-day Bogota, among the oldest human settlements in Colombia.
Paleo-Indians at Tequendama develop stone tools and forage the highland savanna of the Bogota plateau.
Communities along the Caribbean coast at Puerto Hormiga produce some of the earliest pottery in the Americas.
The San Agustin culture begins carving monumental stone statues and building elaborate burial mounds in the southern highlands.
The Tierradentro culture develops underground hypogea tombs decorated with geometric paintings.
The Zenu culture builds vast hydraulic canal systems to manage flooding in the Caribbean lowlands.
Quimbaya goldsmiths perfect sophisticated metalworking and tumbaga alloys in the central Cauca region.
Tairona societies flourish with stone-built towns in the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta.
The Muisca confederation rises on the Andean plateaus, organized under the rival rulers the Zipa and the Zaque.
Alonso de Ojeda and Amerigo Vespucci sail along the Caribbean coast, the first Europeans to reach present-day Colombia.
Rodrigo de Bastidas founds Santa Marta, the oldest surviving Spanish city in Colombia.
Pedro de Heredia founds Cartagena de Indias, which becomes a major port and slave-trading center.
Gonzalo Jimenez de Quesada conquers the Muisca and founds Santa Fe de Bogota, fueling the El Dorado legend.
The Real Audiencia of Santa Fe de Bogota is established to administer Spanish colonial rule.
The Viceroyalty of New Granada is created with Bogota as its capital, uniting much of northern South America.
The Comunero Revolt erupts in Socorro against Spanish taxes, an early sign of colonial discontent.
Bogota's open council deposes the viceroy on 20 July, the date marking Colombia's declaration of independence.
Spanish forces under Pablo Morillo reconquer New Granada during the brutal Reign of Terror.
Simon Bolivar's victory at the Battle of Boyaca secures independence and leads to the creation of Gran Colombia.
The Congress of Cucuta adopts a constitution uniting Colombia, Venezuela, and Ecuador as Gran Colombia.
Gran Colombia dissolves as Venezuela and Ecuador secede, leaving the Republic of New Granada.
Slavery is abolished throughout New Granada under President Jose Hilario Lopez.
The Rionegro Constitution establishes the federalist United States of Colombia.
A centralist constitution creates the Republic of Colombia, remaining in force until 1991.
The Thousand Days' War, a devastating civil war between Liberals and Conservatives, begins.
Panama secedes from Colombia with United States backing to enable construction of the Panama Canal.
The Banana Massacre sees the army kill striking United Fruit Company workers in Cienaga.
Colombia becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
The assassination of Liberal leader Jorge Eliecer Gaitan sparks the Bogotazo riots and the era known as La Violencia.
General Gustavo Rojas Pinilla seizes power in Colombia's only successful 20th-century military coup.
The Liberal and Conservative parties form the power-sharing National Front, ending La Violencia.
The Marxist guerrilla groups FARC and ELN are founded, beginning decades of internal armed conflict.
Novelist Gabriel Garcia Marquez wins the Nobel Prize in Literature, cementing Colombia's magical realism.
The M-19 guerrillas storm the Palace of Justice and the Armero volcanic disaster kills over 23,000 people.
Medellin Cartel violence peaks with the assassination of presidential candidate Luis Carlos Galan.
A new constitution drafted by an elected assembly enshrines rights, pluralism, and judicial reforms.
Drug lord Pablo Escobar is killed by security forces in Medellin, weakening the cocaine cartels.
The United States and Colombia launch Plan Colombia to combat drug trafficking and insurgency.
Alvaro Uribe is elected president and intensifies the military campaign against the FARC.
Operation Jaque rescues hostage Ingrid Betancourt and others held by the FARC.
The government and the FARC sign a peace accord ending over fifty years of war; President Santos wins the Nobel Peace Prize.
Ivan Duque is elected president amid debate over implementation of the peace agreement.
Nationwide protests erupt over a proposed tax reform, exposing deep social and economic grievances.
Gustavo Petro becomes Colombia's first leftist president, with Francia Marquez as the first Afro-Colombian vice president.
